title: SEGMENT 2 — The many reasons people respond to drugs differently

lines.text: SEGMENT 2 — The many reasons people respond to drugs differently
Before we go deep into the DNA, I want to spend a few minutes giving you the full menu of reasons people respond differently to the same medicine.
Because if you walk away thinking it's all genetics, you'll be wrong, and you'll miss things in the clinic that matter.
Genetics is the biggest single explanatory variable for a lot of drugs, but it lives in a noisy environment of other variables, and good prescribing requires considering all of them.
So here's the list, roughly in order of how variable they are across patients.
Genetics.
The big one for this lecture, and we'll spend the rest of the time on it.
Inherited differences in the genes coding for drug-metabolizing enzymes, drug transporters, and drug targets.
Stable across your entire life.
Easy to measure once.
Predictive for many drugs.
Age.
Newborns and very young children have immature liver enzymes.

Their CYP450 system — the metabolic kitchen we'll spend the next several segments inside — doesn't reach adult activity levels until somewhere between age two and adolescence, depending on the specific enzyme.
Elderly patients, on the other end, have declining liver mass, declining kidney function, declining metabolic capacity.
Both extremes are vulnerable to drug accumulation.
A standard adult dose of morphine in a one-year-old can be lethal.
A standard adult dose of a benzodiazepine in a ninety-year-old can put them in a confused, fall-prone state for days.
Sex.

Men and women metabolize many drugs differently, partly because of body composition — women on average have a higher fraction of body fat, which changes the volume of distribution for lipophilic drugs — and partly because of hormonal modulation of liver enzymes.
Estrogen, in particular, modulates several CYP enzymes.
This is why women have, statistically, more adverse drug reactions than men for many drug classes.
It's also one of the reasons drug doses, when first approved, are often calibrated to men and then quietly adjusted later for women.
Body weight and body composition.
Important, but cruder than people often think.

A 120-kilogram patient and a 50-kilogram patient given the same dose will have quite different blood concentrations of a water-soluble drug, but the relationship is not always linear, because the drug doesn't necessarily distribute into fat tissue.
Kidney function.
Many drugs are cleared by the kidneys.
If your kidneys aren't working well — chronic kidney disease, acute injury, age-related decline — those drugs build up.
Renal dosing tables exist for a reason.
Get the dosing wrong on something like vancomycin or digoxin in a patient with bad kidneys, and you have a serious problem on your hands within a day or two.
Liver function.
Most drugs are metabolized by the liver.
Cirrhosis, hepatitis, fatty liver, all reduce metabolic capacity.
The same dose of the same drug in a healthy liver and a cirrhotic liver are not the same exposure to the body.
The cirrhotic patient gets, effectively, more drug per dose.
Disease state.
Inflammation itself, surprisingly, alters drug metabolism.
When you're sick — really sick, with significant systemic inflammation — your liver downregulates many of its metabolic enzymes.
Patients in the ICU with sepsis often metabolize their drugs much more slowly than they would if they were healthy, which is part of why ICU pharmacology is its own demanding subspecialty.
Other drugs.
Drug-drug interactions are an enormous source of variability.
Some drugs induce the metabolic enzymes — they turn the engine up — so they make other drugs clear faster.
Some drugs inhibit the metabolic enzymes — they turn the engine down — so they make other drugs hang around longer.
Grapefruit juice, famously, contains compounds that inhibit a specific liver enzyme, CYP3A4, and that's why certain medications carry a warning not to take them with grapefruit.
A glass of grapefruit juice with the wrong statin can raise the statin's blood concentration five or tenfold, with predictable consequences for your muscles.
Diet and lifestyle.
Smokers metabolize certain drugs faster because compounds in cigarette smoke induce a specific CYP enzyme.
Heavy drinkers chronically induce another.
People on extreme high-fat or high-protein diets shift their metabolism.
Even when you take a drug — fasting versus with food — matters for absorption.
Adherence.
Trivial, but in practice the biggest reason a drug "doesn't work.
" Patients miss doses.
Patients take them at the wrong time.
Patients stop taking them when they feel better, or when they don't, or when the bottle gets put in the wrong drawer.
Now, all of those matter.
But here's the point of this segment.

Even after you control for every single one of those variables — same age, same sex, same weight, same kidneys, same liver, same diet, same other drugs, perfect adherence — you still see massive variation in drug response across patients.
There is a residual, irreducible variability that those non-genetic factors don't explain.
And that residual is, in many cases, the genetics.
For some drugs the genetic contribution is small.
For others — warfarin, clopidogrel, codeine, certain chemotherapies — the genetics dominates.
That's where pharmacogenomics earns its keep, and that's where pre-prescription genotyping has clinical impact you can measure in lives saved.
So that's the framing.
Genetics is one variable among many, but for the drug-gene pairs we care about most, it's the one variable that, if you knew it, would change what you prescribed.
